inline_review:
commit 82a85e05337570f35344420a1df263eefce1c019
Author: Brendan Jackman <jackmanb@google.com>
mm: Change gfp_t to unsigned long

This patch widens gfp_t to unsigned long on 64-bit systems to provide room
for upcoming features to escape GFP bit scarcity. It updates internal printk
formats and kernel module parameters to accommodate the new type size.

> diff --git a/include/linux/types.h b/include/linux/types.h
> index 7e71d260763c..a8490c1381f9 100644
> --- a/include/linux/types.h
> +++ b/include/linux/types.h
[ ... ]
> @@ -160,7 +160,7 @@ typedef u64 dma_addr_t;
>  typedef u32 dma_addr_t;
>  #endif
>  
> -typedef unsigned int __bitwise gfp_t;
> +typedef unsigned long __bitwise gfp_t;

Since the goal is to allow more than 31 flags, will the definition of
__GFP_BITS_MASK in include/linux/gfp_types.h cause undefined behavior?

It is currently defined using 1 << __GFP_BITS_SHIFT. Since 1 is a 32-bit
signed integer, shifting by 31 or more causes overflow. Should this be
updated to use 1UL or 1ULL?

Also, will this cause silent truncation in userspace tools?

For example, the perf kmem tool reads gfp_flags from the trace event into an
unsigned int and stores it in struct gfp_flag as an unsigned int. Any
new flags occupying the upper 32 bits might be silently truncated.

[ ... ]
> diff --git a/lib/test_lockup.c b/lib/test_lockup.c
> index c3fd87d6c2dd..7adc03ac0b39 100644
> --- a/lib/test_lockup.c
> +++ b/lib/test_lockup.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -130,7 +130,7 @@ module_param(alloc_pages_order, uint, 0400);
>  MODULE_PARM_DESC(alloc_pages_order, "page order to allocate");
>  
>  static gfp_t alloc_pages_gfp = GFP_KERNEL;
> -module_param_unsafe(alloc_pages_gfp, uint, 0400);
> +module_param_unsafe(alloc_pages_gfp, unsigned long, 0400);

Does this cause a build failure?

The module_param macro family relies on preprocessor token concatenation to
generate variable names like param_check_##type. Passing a multi-word type
like unsigned long generates invalid C identifiers. Should this use the
ulong alias instead?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Build failure due to macro expansion syntax error with `unsigned long` in `module_param_unsafe`.","severity":"Low","severity_explanation":"1. The patch passes the multi-word type `unsigned long` as the type argument to `module_param_unsafe(alloc_pages_gfp, unsigned long, 0400)`. 
2. The `module_param` macro family relies on preprocessor token concatenation (`##type`) to generate internal kernel API function and variable names, such as `param_check_##type`.
3. Passing `unsigned long` causes the preprocessor to generate invalid C identifiers like `param_check_unsigned long`, leading to a fatal compilation error. The kernel provides the `ulong` alias specifically to avoid this issue. Build issues are classified as Low severity."},{"problem":"Silent truncation of 64-bit `gfp_t` values in `perf kmem` tool.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. The `perf kmem` tool in `tools/perf/builtin-kmem.c` reads `gfp_flags` from the trace event using `unsigned int gfp_flags = evsel__intval(evsel, sample, "gfp_flags")`.
2. It stores these flags internally in `struct gfp_flag` which is defined with `unsigned int flags;`.
3. Since `gfp_t` is now a 64-bit unsigned long on 64-bit systems, any new GFP flags occupying the upper 32 bits will be silently truncated by `perf kmem`, resulting in incorrect trace output and failed flag matching. Issues in userspace tools like `perf` are classified as Medium severity."},{"problem":"Latent undefined behavior in `__GFP_BITS_MASK` prevents using >= 31 GFP flags, defeating the patch's purpose.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The patch widens `gfp_t` to 64 bits on 64-bit systems to allow upcoming features to "escape GFP bit scarcity" (i.e., defining more than 31 flags).
2. However, `include/linux/gfp_types.h` defines the valid bit mask as `#define __GFP_BITS_MASK ((__force gfp_t)((1 << __GFP_BITS_SHIFT) - 1))`.
3. The constant `1` is a 32-bit signed integer. When `__GFP_BITS_SHIFT` reaches 31, `1 << 31` causes signed integer overflow (undefined behavior), and `__GFP_BITS_SHIFT >= 32` results in further undefined behavior. This will result in an incorrect mask that silently drops upper bits, defeating the entire purpose of the patch. The `1` must be changed to `1UL` or `1ULL`."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 82a85e05337570f35344420a1df263eefce1c019
